questions.scenario: The team wants a dashboard tile showing whether checkout is getting worse during a release. They need request count, failed request count, and average duration for `ca-devpolaris-orders-prod`. Which data type fits that tile?

options.text: Metrics, because they summarize counts and timing over time.
Logs, because a dashboard should show every individual checkout message.
Traces, because one trace is enough to show release-wide health.
Tags, because tags summarize runtime performance.

questions.explanation: Metrics are built for counts, rates, latency, and trends. Logs and traces explain individual events after the metric tells the team where to look.

questions.scenario: The failed request count is high for five minutes, then returns to normal. The alert fires once and sends orders on-call an email. What makes this alert useful instead of noisy?

options.text: It has a clear threshold, evaluation window, severity, and receiver group tied to an action the team can take.
It fires for every single failed request, even when retries succeed.
It uses the longest possible retention period so notifications are easier to audit.
It sends to every engineering team because more people means faster diagnosis.

questions.explanation: A useful alert tells the right humans about a meaningful signal. Too many low-value notifications train people to ignore alerts.
